title: CHAPTER 9 —
WORKING WITH SPREADSHEETS

Chapter 9 — Working with Spreadsheets — That address system is not decoration. It is the language in which formulas are written. When you type =D47*F2, you are

beats.text: A spreadsheet is a grid. Columns run left to right, labeled with letters: A, B, C, and eventually AA, AB after Z. Rows run top to bottom, labeled with numbers: 1, 2, 3. A cell's address , its reference , is the column letter plus the row number. The cell in column A, row 1, is A1. The cell in column G, row 47, is G47. That address system is not decoration. It is the language in which formulas are written.

segments.label: 01 / 12 · HOOK

segments.section: THE DOLLAR SIGN AND WHAT IT DO
title: THE DOLLAR SIGN AND
WHAT IT DOES
segments.body_html:
—
ShowingE1 — Showing: E1 — what it means applied to the chapter example.
$E$1 — $E$1 — what it means applied to the chapter example.
$E1 — $E1 — what it means applied to the chapter example.

beats.text: The fix is an absolute reference, written with dollar signs: $E$1. When you write =D2$E$1 and copy it to row 3, the D2 becomes D3 , still relative, still tracking the row , but $E$1 stays exactly as it is. Row 50 gives you =D50$E$1. The D reference travels; the E reference stays locked. The dollar sign syntax is a bit odd to look at until you understand what each dollar sign is doing.

segments.label: 02 / 12 · THE DOLLAR SIGN AND WHAT IT DO

title: FORMULAS AND
FUNCTIONS
segments.body_html:
A formula starts with an equals sign. The equals sign is the signal to the program: this is an instruction, not text. Af

Formulas and Functions — The arithmetic operators are familiar: + for addition, - for subtraction, ` for multiplication (the × symbol is not on a

beats.text: A formula starts with an equals sign. The equals sign is the signal to the program: this is an instruction, not text. After it, you write the arithmetic. The arithmetic operators are familiar: + for addition, - for subtraction, ` for multiplication (the times symbol is not on a standard keyboard), / for division. =5+3 adds five and three. =A1+A2 adds the contents of those two cells. =A1+A2-A3A4/A5` follows the standard order of operations , multiplication and division before addition and subtraction.

segments.label: 03 / 12 · FORMULAS AND FUNCTIONS

segments.section: WHY THE FORMAT IS NOT THE VALU
title: WHY THE FORMAT IS
NOT THE VALUE.
segments.body_html:
—
ShowingGeneral — Showing: General — what it means applied to the chapter example.
Number — Number — what it means applied to the chapter example.
Currency — Currency — what it means applied to the chapter example.

beats.text: A cell contains a value. Separately, a cell has a format that controls how that value is displayed. These are independent, and confusing them causes specific problems. The number 0.15 is the same number whether you display it as 0.15, as 15 percent, or as $0.15. The underlying value is identical. The format is the presentation layer. When you format a cell as a percentage, you are telling the program to display the number multiplied by 100 with a percent sign appended.

segments.label: 04 / 12 · WHY THE FORMAT IS NOT THE VALU

segments.section: THE THREE ERRORS THAT ARE HARD
title: THE THREE ERRORS THAT ARE
HARD TO SEE.
segments.body_html:
Every spreadsheet that has been used long enough has at least one formula error that nobody noticed. The errors are hard

The Three Errors That Are Hard to See — The first is a drifted reference. You copy a formula and one of the cell references adjusts when it should not, or does

beats.text: Every spreadsheet that has been used long enough has at least one formula error that nobody noticed. The errors are hard to see because the formula looks syntactically correct and produces a number , just the wrong number. The first is a drifted reference. You copy a formula and one of the cell references adjusts when it should not, or does not adjust when it should.

segments.label: 05 / 12 · THE THREE ERRORS THAT ARE HARD

title: BUILDING A
MODEL.
segments.body_html:
These concepts — relative and absolute references, functions, formatting — combine into a pattern that underlies every s

Building a Model — The pattern is: data in rows, calculations in columns to the right, summaries below or in a separate area. Each row is a

beats.text: These concepts , relative and absolute references, functions, formatting , combine into a pattern that underlies every spreadsheet model you will encounter in a workplace. The pattern is: data in rows, calculations in columns to the right, summaries below or in a separate area. Each row is a thing , an order, a customer, a product, a day. Each column is a property of that thing , quantity, price, date, status. The calculation columns combine the data columns with formulas.

segments.label: 06 / 12 · BUILDING A MODEL

title: CHAPTER SUMMARY
segments.body_html:
A spreadsheet is a grid of cells, each with an address: column letter plus row number. A formula is an instruction that

Chapter Summary — A relative reference (like D2) adjusts when you copy a formula to a different row or column — the address tracks where t

beats.text: A spreadsheet is a grid of cells, each with an address: column letter plus row number. A formula is an instruction that refers to addresses, not to specific numbers. When the values in those addresses change, the formula recalculates automatically. A relative reference (like D2) adjusts when you copy a formula to a different row or column , the address tracks where the formula moves. An absolute reference (like $D$2) stays fixed when you copy. Use relative references for values that vary row by row.

segments.label: 07 / 12 · CHAPTER SUMMARY

title: EXERCISES
segments.body_html:

is stored in cell G1
→
6%
of revenue
→
8.75%
tax rate embedded directly in the formula

beats.text: 9.1 (Formulas contain addresses, not numbers.) In a blank spreadsheet, type the number 5 in cell A1 and the number 3 in cell A2. In cell A3, type the formula =A1+A2. Note the result. Now change the value in A1 to 8. What happens to A3? Explain in one sentence why A3 updated automatically, using the language of addresses rather than the language of numbers. 9.2 (Relative vs. absolute , predict before you test.) You have this formula in cell F2: =D2*$E$1.

beats.text: The ideas in this chapter didn't appear from nowhere. Dan Bricklin was working on VisiCalc, the first electronic spreadsheet , the program that single-handedly made the personal computer worth buying for business in 1979 decades before most people had heard of spreadsheets. Here's a prompt to find out more , and then make it better.

Prompts — Create a single-file D3 v7 HTML chart using a quantitative y-axis with a zero baseline, 4-6 labeled categories on the x-

beats.text: Create a single-file D3 v7 HTML annotated example using 4-6 labeled nodes or panels, directional connectors where sequence matters, direct labels on every mark, one primary red emphasis mark, neutral supporting marks, and no unlabeled boxes. The figure should represent: Three rows of a spreadsheet showing F2 containing =D2E2, F3 containing =D3E3, F50 containing =D50*E50 , with arrows showing how the row numbers in the references adjust automatically as the formula is copied down, while the column letters stay the same.
